facts_disclosed: Uses Discount Tire Direct as supplier

turns.content: hey so I run a tire and auto shop and Im trying to figure out if Im getting ripped on my inventory costs. is there a way to calculate what my markup should be on tires

Sure thing. The standard markup on tires in the independent shop world usually runs between 35-50% over your wholesale cost, depending on the brand and tire type. Here's a quick way to think about it:

- **Economy tires**: 40-50% markup (customers are price-shopping these, but your cost is low)
- **Mid-range tires**: 35-45% markup (bread and butter, most volume)
- **Premium/performance tires**: 25-35% markup (higher dollar amount per unit even at lower percentage)

To calculate: take your wholesale cost, multiply by 1.4 for a 40% markup. So a tire you buy at $80 wholesale, you'd sell at $112 before mounting and balancing fees.

Who are you buying through, if you don't mind me asking? That affects pricing a lot.
I order most of my inventory through Discount Tire Direct, they give me decent wholesale pricing. but I keep looking at the numbers and feeling like somethings off

Discount Tire Direct is a solid supplier — they're one of the bigger wholesale operations so the pricing is generally competitive. But "decent" and "best" aren't the same thing.

Here's what I'd suggest: pull your last 3 months of invoices from them and make a simple spreadsheet. For your top 10 best-selling tire models, list out:

1. Your wholesale cost per unit
2. What you're selling it for
3. Your actual markup percentage

That'll tell you fast if you're leaving money on the table or if your gut feeling is right. Do you track that stuff anywhere right now, or is it mostly paper invoices?
man I got a filing cabinet full of invoices and a notebook. I know thats bad. I keep meaning to get organized but the days just fly by

That's more common than you'd think for independent shops. You're not alone there.

Here's a realistic starting point that won't eat your whole week: just pick your top 5 tires by volume — the ones you sell the most. Pull the last invoice for each from Discount Tire Direct and write down the cost. Then check what you charged customers for those same tires in the last month.

Five tires, maybe 30 minutes of digging. That alone will tell you whether your margins are healthy or bleeding. You can always expand from there.

Want me to put together a simple template you could print out and fill in by hand? Sometimes pen and paper beats a spreadsheet if that's what actually gets done.
yeah actually that would be real helpful. something simple I can just fill in

Here you go — just copy this into a Word doc or I can format it however works for you:

---

**TIRE MARGIN TRACKER**

Date: ___________

| Tire Brand/Model | Size | Wholesale Cost | My Sale Price | Markup $ | Markup % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. _____________ | ____ | $_______ | $_______ | $_______ | _____% |
| 2. _____________ | ____ | $_______ | $_______ | $_______ | _____% |
| 3. _____________ | ____ | $_______ | $_______ | $_______ | _____% |
| 4. _____________ | ____ | $_______ | $_______ | $_______ | _____% |
| 5. _____________ | ____ | $_______ | $_______ | $_______ | _____% |

**Markup % formula:** (Sale Price - Wholesale Cost) / Wholesale Cost x 100

---

Fill in the first four columns, then do the math for the last two. If any of those markup percentages are under 30%, that's a red flag worth looking at.
appreciate it. now heres the thing, I also pay for mounting balancing and disposal fees on my end. should I be factoring those into the cost or charging separately

Both approaches work, but here's what I'd recommend: charge them as separate line items on the customer's invoice.

Reason being:

1. **Transparency** — customers see the tire price and know the service fees are standard. Nobody likes feeling like costs are hidden.
2. **Margin protection** — if you bake disposal and mounting into the tire price, you eat those costs when they go up. Separate line items let you adjust without changing your advertised tire price.
3. **Competitive pricing** — when people call around asking "how much for a set of 225/65R17s," your tire price stays competitive. Everyone charges mounting and balancing on top.

Typical independent shop rates:
- Mounting & balancing: $15-25 per tire
- Tire disposal/environmental fee: $3-5 per tire

For your own margin tracking though, yes — factor in what those cost YOU as part of your true cost per tire. If you're paying $2 per tire for disposal, that's real money over a year.
thats good thinking. I been rolling disposal into the price and I bet thats eating into things more than I realize

Yeah, that adds up fast. Say you're doing even 30 tires a week and eating $3-4 per tire on disposal — that's roughly $400-500 a month just walking out the door. Over a year you're looking at $5,000+ that could be on your bottom line instead.

Easy fix too. Just add a line item on your invoices starting tomorrow. Most customers don't even blink at a disposal fee — they see it everywhere. It's expected.

Might be worth a quick look at your mounting and balancing charges too while you're at it. Make sure those have kept up with what the market's charging in your area.
yeah I need to do a whole pricing audit honestly. alright man this was helpful. gonna start pulling those invoices tonight

Sounds like a plan. Start with those top 5 tires and the disposal fee change — don't try to overhaul everything at once or it won't get done. Small moves.

Once you've got that margin tracker filled in, feel free to come back and we can look at the numbers together. Might spot some easy wins.

Good luck with the invoices tonight.
